serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Nasi Goreng Sambal Cendawan is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served in Malaysian and Singaporean eateries.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice, Malaysian style) — IMR Malaysia MyFCD; supplemented with HPB Singapore Foodo data for mushroom sambal fried rice variant
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Nasi Goreng Sambal Cendawan found in MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD 'Nasi Goreng' (plain fried rice, Malaysian style) and cross-referenced with HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice' entries. Mushroom content contributes modest additional dietary fibre and micronutrients. Sambal paste adds sodium and fat. Values represent a reasonable composite estimate for a mushroom sambal fried rice dish as served in Singapore/Malaysian hawker and restaurant contexts.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Fried Rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nasi Goreng Sambal Cendawan. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white rice (GI ~64–72, mean ~68) and fried rice variants (GI ~55–65). A value of 64 is used as a conservative estimate for a mixed fried rice dish with vegetables and sambal, which moderates the GI slightly relative to plain white rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 76.5) / 100 ≈ 49.0 g (based on 300 g serving with 25.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from sambal paste and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard plate (approximately 150 g) due to the high sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g, which can contribute significantly to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from refined white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the white rice base produces a medium-to-high glycemic response (GI ~64) and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~49 g GL), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion control to approximately 150 g and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content and elevated sodium from sambal and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the moderate fat content (5.8 g per 100 g) and high sodium level (420 mg per 100 g) in this dish; choosing a reduced-oil preparation and limiting portion size to 150–200 g can help manage saturated fat and sodium intake within cardiovascular dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Goreng Sambal Cendawan is a Malaysian and Singaporean fried rice dish prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a hot wok with sambal chilli paste, mushrooms (cendawan), shallots, garlic, and cooking oil, typically finished with soy sauce and sometimes egg. The dish has a bold, spicy, and savoury flavour profile with a slightly smoky wok-hei aroma. The mushrooms contribute an earthy umami depth and a soft, meaty texture that contrasts with the firm rice grains. Sambal, a cooked chilli-based condiment foundational to Malay and Indonesian cooking, provides heat, sweetness, and complexity. The dish is commonly served as a main meal at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, kopitiams, and casual restaurants. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~168 kcal per 100 g) with a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile (25.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) and moderate fat (5.8 g per 100 g) from cooking oil and sambal. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to sambal paste and soy sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), with a high glycemic load per standard serving, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The mushroom component adds dietary fibre (1.4 g per 100 g), potassium, and B vitamins. The dish is generally halal and can be prepared vegetarian by omitting egg. It is a culturally significant comfort food in the Malay culinary tradition and is widely available across Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Nasi Goreng Sambal Cendawan was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest available Malaysian fried rice entries in MyFCD and cross-referenced with HPB Foodo fried rice data. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried rice data. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Food group corrected from input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Grains and staples' as this is a rice-based main dish, not a condiment. Vitamin B12 is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this plant-forward dish in the source databases; the dish is predominantly plant-based and rice-based with negligible B12 content. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo source entries for fried rice variants. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases for this dish. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases for this dish. Trans fat is 0.0 confirmed as negligible in home-style and hawker-style wok cooking using vegetable oils. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entries; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 300 g serving size: (64 × 76.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 49.0. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false as egg is commonly included; is_vegetarian set to true as the dish can be and is commonly prepared without meat. Halal status is true as served at halal-certified establishments including those listed in the input.
